narrative.elevator-pitch: I'm a builder-architect hybrid. Most Principal Architects either think strategically or write code—I do both. I've stood up five new functions at Google and Microsoft, including the program that led to Google Cloud Migration Center. I designed McDonald's enterprise AI architecture AND built agents that are running in production. I can see the big picture and I can ship.

evidence.proof: 4 McDonald's agents deployed (GCP Champs automation, TPM project analysis, capability modeling, architecture research)
WaivePath 8-agent orchestration system built from scratch
Food Network Windows 8 app (85-95% of production code)
Agentient multi-agent system with MCP server integration

differentiator.talking-point: "I can architect solutions I actually know how to build. My hands-on agent development at Agentient directly informs my enterprise architecture work at McDonald's."

evidence.proof: GCP Champs: 154 direct + 166 pull-through participants, 7X completion rate
Stratozone program → acquired → Google Cloud Migration Center

differentiator.talking-point: "I deliberately pursue emerging functions where I can build from zero. The trade-off is organizational volatility—these functions get restructured. The benefit is I've stood up five new practices, each time creating infrastructure that outlasted my tenure."

evidence.proof: Tribune: Workshop attendee → CEO presentation in weeks
AON: Competitive win against Google, invited to Rotterdam CTO conference
McDonald's CIO adoption of architecture, VP adoption of Agent Maria

differentiator.talking-point: "I research clients' strategic initiatives deeply before engaging. This consultative approach opens doors that pure sales can't reach."

evidence.proof: Served as SME on Stratozone acquisition team
Conducted build vs. buy analysis for GPM Compute
Identified product gaps, negotiated vendor roadmap commitments
Connected M&A team with target CEO
Product became Google Cloud Migration Center

differentiator.talking-point: "I drove the acquisition recommendation that became Google Cloud Migration Center— consolidated competing partner programs, conducted build vs. buy analysis, negotiated vendor commitments, and connected M&A with the CEO."

question.prompt: You've been at McDonald's less than a year. What have you actually delivered?
question.concern: Short tenure with potentially thin concrete achievements
question.recommended-response: McDonald's engaged me to design their AI platform architecture, which I delivered and the CIO adopted. But I also built working agents—the GCP Champs automation serving 150+ participants, the TPM project analysis tool that our VP uses, and architecture research tooling for the EA team. I believe architects should prove their designs work by building pieces of them.

proof-points.proof: 4 AI agents deployed and in active use
325 use cases synthesized into 22 reusable patterns

question.prompt: You had multiple role changes at Google. What happened?
question.concern: Pattern of restructuring rather than advancement
question.recommended-response: I deliberately pursue zero-to-one opportunities—building new functions in emerging spaces. The trade-off is organizational volatility. Field CTO got centralized to engineering because Google wanted VPs with 20+ years of industry experience. Value Engineering I scaled 10X, then they brought in specialists with 25 years of specific experience to take it further. Industry Enablement was deprioritized in a strategic shift. But the Stratozone program I built became Google Cloud Migration Center. I build things that last even when my role doesn't.
question.reframe: "Restructured out" → "Built something valuable enough that it needed its own organization"

question.prompt: Are you actually technical, or just a strategist who makes decks?
question.concern: Principal Architect title often means paper architecture only
question.recommended-response: I wrote 85% of the production code for the Food Network Windows 8 app—that was a flagship launch showcase, C# on a new platform with limited documentation and a hard deadline. At Agentient, I'm building multi-agent systems with MCP integration and cross-platform context management. At McDonald's, I use Copilot Studio and Power Automate for practical automation. I operate at whatever technical level solves the problem.
question.calibration-note: McDonald's agents are low-code (Copilot Studio, Power Automate, Rovo). Don't conflate with distributed systems engineering. Agentient work is more sophisticated (MCP server, multi-agent orchestration).

question.prompt: You claim $1.5B pipeline. How do you attribute that to yourself?
question.concern: Large numbers at big companies often reflect proximity, not causation
question.recommended-response: When I took over Value Engineering, my predecessor had built a partnership with Stratozone running at about $150M pipeline. I made the decision to scale it globally— flew to 20 cities to train the sales force, renegotiated the partner contract to add capabilities at no cost, and reinvested savings into the enablement program. I also consolidated a second partner (CloudPhysics) under the same umbrella. In 9 months, the program scaled to $1.5B. The causal chain is clear: I built the infrastructure that enabled the field to qualify and quantify deals at scale.
question.better-framing: "Scaled Google Cloud's value engineering program from $150M to $1.5B pipeline (10X growth) by training the global sales force across 20 cities, renegotiating partner contracts, and standardizing TCO methodology."

question.prompt: You mention a $3.3B opportunity that didn't close. How is that an achievement?
question.concern: Claiming credit for deals that didn't happen
question.recommended-response: The $3.3B demonstrates capability, not outcome. My job was business development— identify opportunities, secure executive access, build strategic cases. I went from a technical workshop to CEO presentation within weeks through self-initiated market research. The tablet deal didn't close due to device quality issues outside my control. What I did convert was a major Windows Azure partnership that saved Tribune $1.5M annually. The Azure deal was the actual win; the tablet opportunity shows how I find and pursue strategic possibilities.

question.prompt: Your startup ventures don't seem successful. Why should I value that experience?
question.recommended-response: WaivePath taught me that target market selection matters more than product quality— we built for salespeople who don't buy learning products instead of sales enablement professionals who do. That's a $50K lesson I won't repeat. The technical work was real: I designed an 8-agent content orchestration system from scratch, built the entire platform, and shipped to market. Agentient applies those lessons—extensive market validation, founder interviews, and I own 100% so partner dynamics can't derail execution. The hands-on development keeps my technical skills sharp, which makes me a better enterprise architect.
question.reframe: "Failed startup" → "Shipped product, learned market validation lessons I'm applying now"

question.prompt: What does "analyzed 325 use cases" actually mean?
question.concern: Large numbers might be superficial taxonomy work
question.recommended-response: We ran discovery workshops across 8 business functions to identify AI opportunities. I took 325 raw ideas, consolidated duplicates, wrote problem statements and solution alternatives for each, then analyzed the full portfolio to identify 22 reusable architectural patterns. It's portfolio synthesis and pattern extraction—not deep technical design on 325 individual solutions. The value is helping McDonald's prioritize where limited AI engineering resources should focus for maximum impact.
question.better-framing: "Led AI opportunity discovery across 8 business functions, synthesizing 325 use cases into 22 reusable architectural patterns; developing prioritization framework to maximize engineering team impact."

question.prompt: You say you "managed" 88 people. Did you actually manage them?
question.recommended-response: Let me be precise: I led a cross-functional program with 88 contributors across 10 workstreams through Google's 20% project framework. I recruited, coordinated, and provided performance feedback, but I had no hire/fire authority or budget. The achievement was achieving results through influence rather than positional power— which is often harder than direct management.
question.correction: Always say "led" not "managed" for this program.

vulnerability.concern: Pattern of roles ending via restructure rather than advancement
vulnerability.honest-assessment: True. Zero-to-one roles in emerging functions are inherently volatile. This is a deliberate trade-off, not a failure pattern.
vulnerability.reframe: "I build things that outlast my tenure. The Stratozone program became Google Cloud Migration Center. The enablement standards I created were adopted across Google Cloud GTM. The architecture operating model at McDonald's will guide the organization for years."

proof-of-lasting-impact.proof: Stratozone → Google Cloud Migration Center (permanent product)
Industry BA program → Principal Architect Level 300 accreditation

vulnerability.concern: 10-layer architecture is not implemented; tenure too short for production deployment
vulnerability.honest-assessment: True. Target-state architecture has 2-3 year horizon. Less than 1 year tenure means limited production deployment.
vulnerability.mitigation: Lead with the 4 agents that ARE running. Frame architecture as "validated by working prototypes."
vulnerability.talking-point: "The architecture is a 2-3 year roadmap. What's running today are the agents I built to prove key patterns work—GCP Champs automation, TPM project analysis, architecture research tools."

vulnerability.concern: McDonald's agents are low-code (Copilot Studio), not sophisticated engineering
vulnerability.honest-assessment: True. Copilot Studio and Power Automate are citizen developer tools. Don't conflate with building distributed systems.
vulnerability.calibration: Technical depth ranges from: - Production code (Food Network app, C#) - Sophisticated system architecture (Agentient, MCP, multi-agent) - Practical low-code automation (McDonald's agents) Position as "operating at whatever level solves the problem."

vulnerability.honest-assessment: WaivePath has limited traction. Agentient hasn't launched. This is honest. Don't overclaim.
vulnerability.reframe: "Founder who shipped product, learned hard lessons about market validation and partner selection, applying those lessons to current work."

metric.causal-chain: Inherited $150M run-rate → scaled globally via training (20 cities) + partner consolidation + contract renegotiation → 10X growth

metric.claim: Drove acquisition recommendation that became Google Cloud Migration Center
metric.causal-chain: SME on evaluation team → build vs. buy analysis → gap identification → vendor negotiation → CEO connection → acquisition closed
metric.note: This is undersold. Should be headline of Google section.

metric.issue: Deal didn't close. Opportunity identification ≠ achievement.
metric.reframe: Lead with capability (CEO access in weeks) and actual win (Azure deal, $1.5M savings). Use $3.3B to demonstrate strategic thinking scope, not as outcome.

metric.issue: "Analyzed" is ambiguous. Could mean taxonomy or deep design.
metric.clarification: Portfolio synthesis + pattern extraction (22 patterns), not 325 individual technical designs.
metric.reframe: "Synthesized 325 use cases into 22 reusable architectural patterns"

metric.issue: Were not direct reports. Volunteer 20% project.
metric.correction: Say "led" not "managed." The achievement is influence without authority.

metric.causal-chain: Designed program → executed training → measured outcomes from reports
metric.note: CIO praised program. This is concrete with real metrics.

metric.calibration: Real and running, but small scale: - GCP Champs: ~90 users - TPM/Agent Maria: dozens of executives, dozens of proposals - Architecture tools: handful of EA users
metric.reframe: "Built working agents that validated architecture patterns" rather than "deployed enterprise AI"

never-say.item: "I'm the most knowledgeable person on AI at McDonald's"
"McDonald's is just my income source; my passion is elsewhere"
"I deployed AI agents across McDonald's enterprise" (they're small-scale experiments)
"I achieved a $3.3B deal at Microsoft" (opportunity, not win)
"I managed 88 people at Google" (led program, no hire/fire)

item.reason: Paper architecture isn't achievement at Principal level

item.context: When discussing Agentient alongside corporate work

always-say.item: "I build AI systems professionally and personally. The hands-on work keeps my technical skills sharp, which makes me a better architect."
"I shipped a product, learned hard lessons about market validation and partner selection, and I'm applying those lessons to my current work."
"I deliberately pursue zero-to-one opportunities. The Stratozone program I built became Google Cloud Migration Center—I build things that last."
"I operate at whatever technical level solves the problem—from production code to sophisticated agent architectures to practical automation."
